answer:
**In the Bṛhaspati Smṛti, 'lekhya' (document or writing) is one of the three primary human means of proof (*mānuṣī pramāṇāni*) in legal disputes (*vyavahāra*), alongside witnesses (*sākṣi*) and possession/enjoyment (*bhukti*).**[^1] It is introduced early as a key topic for resolving creditor-debtor claims and other doubtful matters.[^2] A lekhya is defined as a written record containing the local custom (*deśācāra*), date (year, month, fortnight, etc.), interest rate (*vṛddhi*), and signatures (*hastāṅka*) of the debtor (*ṛṇi*), witnesses (*sākṣi*), and scribe (*lekhaka*); it serves as authoritative evidence (*pramāṇa*) in disputes.[^3]

The text elaborates extensively on lekhya in the proof section (1.4–1.6), classifying it hierarchically: superior to witnesses when available (no ordeal or witnesses needed if a valid document exists), and essential for debts, partitions, sales, etc.[^4] It lists **seven worldly documents (*laukikaṃ lekhyaṃ*)**: partition deed (*bhāgapatram*), boundary deed (*sīmāpatram*), gift deed (*dānalekhyaṃ*), sale deed (*krayapatram*), servitude bond (*dāsapatram*), mortgage deed (*ādhilekhyaṃ*), and compact (*saṃvidpatram*).[^5] **Three royal charters (*rājaśāsanam*)** include gift charters (*dānaśāsanam*), favor documents (*prasādalekhyaṃ*), and victory documents (*jayapatram* recording full proceedings).[^6]

Flaws (*doṣa*) invalidate a lekhya, such as those made by incompetent parties (dying, insane, coerced), forged signatures, poor condition (faded, damaged), or single flawed witness/scribe.[^7] Detection involves scrutiny by experts (*lekhyaṃ kuśalāḥ*), reasoning (*yukti*), and sometimes ordeals if ambiguous.[^8] Aged documents (>30 years without use) lose validity, emphasizing active enforcement.[^9]

### Expansion of the Older Smṛti Tradition
Older smṛtis like Manu (8.62–66) prioritize witnesses and briefly note documents as secondary, without classification or forgery rules.[^10] Yājñavalkya (2.83–92) mentions documents (*lekhya*) in sales but lacks detail.[^11] Bṛhaspati expands this into a mature system within its 18-topic *vyavahāra* framework (wealth/violence disputes, four "feet": plaint/reply/evidence/decision), elevating lekhya to equal/superior proof.[^12] This reflects societal evolution toward written contracts, guilds (*śreṇī*), and commerce, with innovations like victory documents for finality and detailed forgery detection—absent in earlier texts—ensuring stability (*sthairya*).[^13] It systematizes *vyavahāra* (e.g., hierarchy: document > witnesses > ordeals), influencing later works like Kātyāyana.[^14]

The Bṛhaspati Smṛti's focus on lekhya underscores writing's role in preventing disputes (*bhrānti*), promoting commerce under royal oversight.[^15]

[^1]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-510297) (witnesses, documents, inference as human proofs).
[^2]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-cb5a36) (Brh_1,1.5).
[^3]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-dc2d5a) (Brh_1,6.3).
[^4]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-44a57a) (Brh_1,4.12); hierarchy in [p-fc9858].
[^5]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-213342)–[p-8fb39a] (Brh_1,6.4–17).
[^6]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-c77410)–[p-944786] (Brh_1,6.20–27).
[^7]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-a9b952)–[p-65f09b] (Brh_1,6.30–42).
[^8]: Bṛhaspati Smṛti, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-c66519) (Brh_1,6.44).

[^10]: Manusmṛti, [Chapter 8](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/08/translation/#p-803bc3).
